sections.summary: Iodinated contrast can precipitate acute kidney injury, and impaired renal clearance of metformin may then lead to metformin-associated lactic acidosis. Metformin is typically held around contrast administration in at-risk patients.
sections.mechanism: Contrast media can cause contrast-induced nephropathy. Because metformin is cleared renally and is not metabolized, reduced kidney function causes metformin to accumulate, promoting lactate buildup and metabolic acidosis.
sections.clinical_effects: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, hyperventilation, and lethargy from lactic acidosis; rising creatinine from contrast nephropathy.
sections.management: In patients with reduced eGFR, withhold metformin at the time of contrast and for 48 hours afterward, resuming only after renal function is confirmed stable. Ensure adequate hydration around the procedure.
sections.monitoring: Check renal function before restarting metformin after contrast exposure, particularly in patients with pre-existing chronic kidney disease.
